Name: PEBL
Built-in Scoring: Partial — many individual tests write a per-session summary file with a crosstable of common dependent variables (e.g., total correct, mean RT per condition), but this is test-by-test, not a unified scoring engine
Results Review / Dashboard: No central dashboard — output is local text/CSV files per session; no online review portal
Interpretation & Normative Data: No built-in norms; published evaluations note that "most PEBL paradigms still lack proper evaluation of their reliability and validity" beyond ~10 tasks with separate validity studies
Notes / Source: ResearchGate PEBL output FAQ; PMC4690381 (reliability/validity study); PMC9882756 (attention tests evaluation)

Name: Self-Regulation Ontology battery
Built-in Scoring: Yes — dedicated expfactory-analysis pipeline computes per-task dependent variables from raw trials (accuracy, RT, drift-diffusion model parameters, delay-discounting rate k, etc.)
Results Review / Dashboard: Results delivered as processed CSVs/R notebooks (e.g., SRO_Retest_Analyses reports); no live participant-facing dashboard
Interpretation & Normative Data: No clinical norms — used for research-construct validation (factor structure, test-retest reliability across 522+ participants), not individual interpretation

Name: Experiment Factory (expfactory)
Built-in Scoring: No built-in scoring beyond whatever the underlying jsPsych task computes per trial (e.g., raw correct/RT fields)
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard; data delivered as flat files from the deployed container
Interpretation & Normative Data: None built in — purely a deployment/container layer

Name: TestMyBrain / Many Brains Project
Built-in Scoring: Yes — each test has a published, test-specific scoring algorithm
Results Review / Dashboard: Yes — the TMB Analysis Toolkit provides downloadable test documentation and a data dictionary per test for researchers to build custom analyses
Interpretation & Normative Data: Yes — reference normative datasets (~1,000 participants, ages 18–89) are provided per test so users can build their own normative models; reliability/factor structure published
Notes / Source: Cohen et al. 2021 J Clin Exp Neuropsychol (DNT Toolkit paper); cogsci.testmybrain.org/dashboard/tmb-analysis-toolkit.html

Name: Flowers Team Cognitive Test Battery
Built-in Scoring: No built-in scoring beyond raw per-trial logs (p5.js front end)
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard beyond whatever the Django admin exposes for consent/survey records
Interpretation & Normative Data: None — research code, not a validated/normed clinical battery

Name: cognitive-battery (thesimonho)
Built-in Scoring: No — outputs a raw response dataframe; scoring logic is left to the researcher
Results Review / Dashboard: No
Interpretation & Normative Data: None

Name: Working Memory Test Battery (Tatool-based)
Built-in Scoring: Likely yes for span tasks — partial-credit unit scoring is the standard approach for complex span tasks and is documented in the JORS paper
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard
Interpretation & Normative Data: No clinical norms, but the JORS paper reports reliability/validity data for the implemented span tasks (research-grade, not clinical norms)

Name: Tatool / Tatool-web
Built-in Scoring: Framework-level only — researchers author their own scoring/feedback logic per task; no fixed scoring engine
Results Review / Dashboard: Yes, partially — Tatool includes an experimenter-facing web interface to monitor participant progress/compliance during longitudinal cognitive training studies
Interpretation & Normative Data: None built in

Name: ICAR (International Cognitive Ability Resource)
Built-in Scoring: Yes — IRT-based ability (theta) estimation using published item discrimination/difficulty parameters, via tools like the R `psych` package's `scoreIrt()` functions
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard — ICAR is an item bank, not a delivery platform
Interpretation & Normative Data: Yes — parameters were estimated in a norming sample of 96,000+ participants, enabling percentile/normative comparison; ICAR16 validated against WAIS-IV for convergent validity
Notes / Source: personality-project.org/r/psych (psych package); ICAR16/WAIS-IV convergent validity study

Name: Niv Lab jsPsych Demos
Built-in Scoring: Yes for the executive-function tests (Raven's, MaRs-IB, digit symbol matching, vocabulary) which compute standard accuracy/RT scores; reinforcement-learning tasks log trial-by-trial choice and outcome data for downstream computational modeling (e.g., RL/Bayesian model fitting) rather than a single summary score
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard — static demo site plus downloadable/forkable task code; researchers self-host and collect their own data
Interpretation & Normative Data: No built-in clinical norms; tasks are designed as building blocks for research studies (several originate from published computational psychiatry/decision-neuroscience paradigms) rather than as a normed assessment battery

Name: jsPsych
Built-in Scoring: No automatic scoring — researchers manually compute a `correct` field in trial callbacks (e.g., via `jsPsych.pluginAPI.compareKeys()`); library provides post-hoc data-filtering utilities (`jsPsych.data.get().filter()`)
Results Review / Dashboard: No dashboard built into the library itself (whatever hosting service is used — e.g., JATOS — may add one)
Interpretation & Normative Data: None

Name: PsychoPy / PsychoJS
Built-in Scoring: Partial — built-in adaptive staircase/QUEST handlers automatically estimate psychophysical thresholds (mean/mode/quantile methods); no scoring for cognitive/clinical tasks
Results Review / Dashboard: No participant-results dashboard in the library itself; Pavlovia gives researchers a basic session/data-management view
Interpretation & Normative Data: None beyond psychophysical threshold statistics

Name: OpenSesame
Built-in Scoring: Partial — separate community add-ons (e.g., OpenSesame Toolbox, Questionnaire Processor) can summarize/score multiple-choice questionnaires, but this isn't core to OpenSesame itself
Results Review / Dashboard: No
Interpretation & Normative Data: None

Name: Expyriment
Built-in Scoring: No
Results Review / Dashboard: No
Interpretation & Normative Data: No

Name: Psychtoolbox-3
Built-in Scoring: No general scoring; ships its own QUEST-style threshold-fitting routines for psychophysics, similar in scope to PsychoPy's
Results Review / Dashboard: No
Interpretation & Normative Data: No

Name: PsyBuilder
Built-in Scoring: No — GUI only generates Psychtoolbox code; scoring is the researcher's responsibility
Results Review / Dashboard: No
Interpretation & Normative Data: No

Name: lab.js
Built-in Scoring: No built-in scoring; exports raw JSON/CSV
Results Review / Dashboard: Partial — the companion Open Lab hosting service gives researchers a basic dashboard for monitoring data collection (session counts, completion), not score interpretation
Interpretation & Normative Data: None

Name: PsyToolkit
Built-in Scoring: Yes — can score survey scales live during the session (real-time subscale/total scoring), enabling immediate participant feedback
Results Review / Dashboard: Yes — a built-in ""quick overview of data"" feature gives basic descriptive review without leaving the platform
Interpretation & Normative Data: Basic — descriptive statistics only via its built-in tools and companion R library; no clinical norms/percentiles built in

Name: MindLogger
Built-in Scoring: Yes — applets can implement automated scoring logic for validated questionnaires via conditional scoring rules in the applet builder
Results Review / Dashboard: Yes — dedicated researcher-facing data dashboard for viewing individual and aggregate participant responses
Interpretation & Normative Data: Limited — no built-in clinical cutoffs/norms beyond whatever scoring logic the applet author encodes (e.g., a PHQ-9 cutoff must be manually built into the applet)

Name: Concerto Platform
Built-in Scoring: Yes, extensively — built-in IRT/CAT scoring algorithms compute theta (ability) estimates and standard errors of measurement live during testing
Results Review / Dashboard: Yes — admin interface for monitoring and reviewing respondent results
Interpretation & Normative Data: Yes — explicitly supports comparison to normative values, automatic report generation, personalized written feedback, and trend/radial plots for multi-dimensional interpretation; the most clinically complete of all platforms reviewed
Notes / Source: PMC7661245 (Concerto + CAT + ML for patient-reported outcomes); ehps.net Concerto introduction article

Name: IAT implementations (multiple independent repos)
Built-in Scoring: Yes — the D-score (Greenwald algorithm) is a standard, well-documented effect-size scoring method; dedicated downstream tools exist separate from test-delivery code (DscoreApp, implicitMeasures R package — 6 D-score variants, implicitApp)
Results Review / Dashboard: Depends on implementation — most delivery repos just export raw latencies; scoring is typically a separate downstream step using the tools above
Interpretation & Normative Data: No formal clinical norms shipped with the open-source code; Project Implicit's own site provides population-level comparison context separately
